global.title: PSS Chat Messaging

coreIdeas.contextPhrase: PSS is the core 1-to-1 messaging primitive — defines the 2–10 s latency budget every chat UX decision reacts to.
GSOC provides the complementary many-to-one sub-second channel used for notifications.
Decision rationale for combining PSS (messages) + GSOC (notifications) — the architectural split.
Full chat architecture: PSS live + ACT-protected chatlog feed for durable history.
Explains why chat history persists in an ACT feed — PSS messages expire with stamp TTL.
Chat topic derivation from sorted participant addresses — the shared-computation trick that removes coordination.
Concrete PSS message flow: trojan chunks into the recipient's neighborhood.
Document sharing inside chat reuses ACT share bundles + GSOC notifications — cross-feature integration.
Decision to cut typing indicators because PSS latency makes them pointless — scope-trim based on the primitive's limits.
Full Bee nodes are a hard requirement for chat because light nodes cannot receive PSS/GSOC.
Public profile feed supplies the keys and overlay addresses PSS + ACT need to address peers.
Pointer to the adapter/src/chat and adapter/src/plugin code that implements this runtime.

global.description: The messaging primitives and decisions behind Connect chat on Swarm: PSS for 1-to-1, GSOC for notifications, ACT-protected feed for history.
global.orientation: Chat in Connect-on-Swarm is built by matching latency to purpose: PSS trojan chunks deliver 1-to-1 encrypted messages, GSOC gives many-to-one sub-second notifications, and an ACT-protected feed persists history past PSS stamp TTLs. This MOC collects the primitives (PSS, GSOC), the operational constraints (full Bee nodes, sorted-topic derivation), the integration points (share bundles, chat code layout), and the decisions (typing indicators cut, PSS+GSOC combination). It is the reference surface for anyone extending the chat runtime.
